name: Proto-Indo-European
region: Pontic-Caspian Steppe (modern Ukraine, southern Russia, Kazakhstan)
speakers_culture: Nomadic pastoralists; hypothesised Yamnaya culture
split_reason: Migrations outward from steppe ~3500–2500 BCE (Yamnaya expansion). Isolation of groups over generations caused dialects to diverge into distinct branches.
interesting_fact: Never written down — reconstructed entirely by comparing its descendants. Linguists can deduce PIE speakers had words for wheel, axle, and horse, placing them after ~3500 BCE.

speech_bubbles.translation: The horse — faster than any man alive.
My feet have crossed the steppe for three moons.
We follow the water east.

name: Proto-Italic
speakers_culture: Pre-Roman Indo-European peoples of Italy
split_reason: Diverged into Old Latin and the Sabellic languages (Oscan, Umbrian, Faliscan).
interesting_fact: Proto-Italic speakers were not Romans — they were the common ancestors of Romans, Oscans, Umbrians, and other Italian peoples who preceded Roman dominance.

name: Old Latin
speakers_culture: Early Romans and Latins of central Italy
split_reason: Standardised into Classical Latin through literary and political centralisation under the Roman Republic.
interesting_fact: The earliest Old Latin inscription is the Duenos inscription (~600 BCE) — found on a ceramic vessel in Rome, its meaning is still debated by scholars.

speech_bubbles.translation: A good man made me — the Duenos inscription, ~600 BCE.

name: Classical Latin

parent_influence.old-latin: direct standardisation by grammarians and literary tradition

region: Roman Republic and Empire, centred on Italy
speakers_culture: Educated Romans; the language of Caesar, Cicero, Virgil, and the Senate
split_reason: Evolved into Vulgar Latin (spoken) while remaining as a frozen literary and ecclesiastical language preserved by the Church.
interesting_fact: Latin never truly died — it continued as the language of the Catholic Church, science, law, and medicine until the 18th century. Scientific names for species are still written in Latin today.

sample_words.meaning: I came, I saw, I conquered — Julius Caesar
the die is cast — Caesar crossing the Rubicon

speech_bubbles.translation: I came, I saw, I conquered — Caesar after the Battle of Zela, 47 BCE.
The die is cast — Caesar crossing the Rubicon, 49 BCE.

name: Vulgar Latin

parent_influence.classical-latin: spoken form; diverged from written Classical Latin across Roman provinces

region: Roman Empire — Western Europe, North Africa, Near East
speakers_culture: Ordinary inhabitants of the Roman Empire
split_reason: Geographic fragmentation of the Roman Empire caused Vulgar Latin dialects to diverge into the Romance languages: French, Spanish, Portuguese, Italian, Romanian, and others.
interesting_fact: Vulgar Latin was never written as a prestige form — it was the spoken tongue. What we know of it comes from Pompeii graffiti, soldiers' letters, and reconstruction from Romance languages.

sample_words.meaning: horse (replaced classical 'equus'; source of French 'cheval', Spanish 'caballo')
head (replaced classical 'caput'; source of French 'tête', Italian 'testa')

speech_bubbles.translation: Either come in or stay out! — Pompeii graffiti, 1st century CE.
Do not touch — another Pompeii warning, 79 CE.

name: Proto-Germanic

parent_influence.proto-indo-european: direct descent; Grimm's Law consonant shift defines the branch

speakers_culture: Early Germanic tribes of northern Europe
split_reason: Geographic separation of tribal groups across northern Europe led to divergence into North, West, and East Germanic branches.
interesting_fact: Grimm's Law is why English 'fish' corresponds to Latin 'piscis', and 'father' to Latin 'pater'. The same systematic consonant shift applies across all Germanic languages.

speech_bubbles.translation: The king speaks. The tribe listens.
The wolf howls — winter is near.

name: Proto-West-Germanic

parent_influence.proto-germanic: direct descent; split from North and East Germanic branches

region: Northern Germany, Netherlands, southern Britain coast
speakers_culture: West Germanic tribal groups including Angles, Saxons, Franks, Frisians
split_reason: Migration and settlement across western Europe separated West Germanic groups, leading to Old English, Old High German, Old Saxon, Old Dutch, and Old Frisian.
interesting_fact: The 5th-century Anglo-Saxon migration to Britain — one of history's great demographic shifts — is the direct cause of this split. The invaders' language became English.

speech_bubbles.translation: We cross the sea. There is land to the west.
Build the house before winter.

name: Old English

parent_influence.proto-west-germanic: direct descent via Anglian and Saxon dialects

speakers_culture: Anglo-Saxons — Angles, Saxons, and Jutes who migrated from northern Germany and Denmark
split_reason: The Norman Conquest of 1066 brought massive French influence, transforming Old English into Middle English over the following two centuries.
interesting_fact: Beowulf, the oldest major literary work in English, was composed in Old English around 700–1000 CE. A modern English speaker cannot understand a single sentence of it without study.

speech_bubbles.translation: Listen! I have a story worth hearing. — opening of Beowulf.
We are English men.

name: Old French (Norman dialect)

parent_influence.old-norse: substrate from Viking settlement of Normandy — place names, nautical vocab

speakers_culture: Normans — descendants of Viking settlers who adopted Romance speech; became the ruling class of England after 1066
split_reason: After the Norman Conquest, Norman French became the prestige language of England for ~200 years, then was absorbed into Middle English.
interesting_fact: Norman French is why English has pairs like cow/beef, pig/pork, sheep/mutton — the Anglo-Saxon peasants tended the animals, the Norman lords ate them.

speech_bubbles.translation: The king commands.
In the name of the law.

name: Middle English

parent_influence.old-english: grammatical structure, core vocabulary (body, nature, basic verbs), syntax
parent_influence.old-french-norman: ~60% of vocabulary, especially law, court, religion, cuisine, fashion

speakers_culture: English people during the medieval period; Norman aristocracy gradually assimilating
split_reason: The Great Vowel Shift (~1400–1700) and standardisation around the London dialect during the printing press era transformed Middle English into Early Modern English.
interesting_fact: Chaucer wrote The Canterbury Tales in Middle English (~1390). A modern reader can learn to read it with some effort — unlike Old English, which requires full study.

speech_bubbles.original: Whan that Aprill with his shoures soote
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Whan that Aprill with his shoures soote
speech_bubbles.translation: When April with its sweet showers — Chaucer, Canterbury Tales, ~1390.

speech_bubbles.original: The knyght was a worthy man.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: The knyght was a worthy man.
speech_bubbles.translation: The knight was a worthy man.

name: Early Modern English

parent_influence.middle-english: direct descent; Great Vowel Shift and printing standardisation

region: England, Wales; beginning of global spread
speakers_culture: Tudor and Stuart England; first English colonists
split_reason: Gradual standardisation produced Modern English by ~1700. The King James Bible (1611) locked in many grammatical forms that persist today.
interesting_fact: Shakespeare wrote in Early Modern English. 'Thou', 'thee', and 'thy' were the singular pronouns — 'you' was originally only plural and formal, like French 'vous'.

speech_bubbles.original: To be, or not to be, that is the question.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: To be, or not to be, that is the question.
speech_bubbles.translation: Shakespeare, Hamlet, ~1600. Already largely readable today.

speech_bubbles.original: In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.
speech_bubbles.translation: King James Bible, 1611.

name: Modern English

parent_influence.early-modern-english: direct descent; continued lexical borrowing from Latin, Greek, French, and global languages

region: Global — originating in Britain, spread via colonialism
speakers_culture: ~380 million native speakers; ~1.5 billion total speakers worldwide
interesting_fact: English has no official regulatory body. It borrows freely from every language it encounters — roughly 80% of English vocabulary is borrowed, from French, Latin, Norse, and hundreds of other languages.

sample_words.meaning: happy accident (coined 1754, from a Persian fairy tale)

speech_bubbles.translation: Neil Armstrong, 1969. English spoken on the moon.
The most widely spoken word on Earth.

name: Proto-North-Germanic
speakers_culture: Early Scandinavian peoples
split_reason: Differentiation of Scandinavian dialects led to Old Norse, which then diverged into Icelandic, Faroese, Norwegian, Swedish, and Danish.
interesting_fact: Proto-North-Germanic is evidenced by early runic inscriptions in the Elder Futhark alphabet from ~150 CE onward — some of the earliest Germanic writing we have.

speech_bubbles.translation: I — carved in stone, 150 CE.

name: Proto-East-Germanic
speakers_culture: East Germanic tribes including Goths, Vandals, Burgundians
split_reason: East Germanic languages are entirely extinct. Gothic is the only one with substantial written records.
interesting_fact: East Germanic is the only Germanic branch entirely extinct — all its languages died without living descendants.

name: Gothic
speakers_culture: Visigoths and Ostrogoths
split_reason: Gothic died out as Goths were absorbed into Roman and later populations. Crimean Gothic survived until the 18th century.
interesting_fact: Bishop Wulfila translated the Bible into Gothic ~350 CE, inventing the Gothic alphabet to do so. The Codex Argenteus — a Gothic Bible written in silver ink on purple vellum — survives in Uppsala, Sweden.

speech_bubbles.translation: In the beginning God created heaven — Gothic Bible, ~350 CE.

name: Crimean Gothic

parent_influence.gothic: direct descent; isolated on Crimean peninsula for ~1000 years

speakers_culture: Goths who remained in Crimea, surrounded by Slavic, Turkic, and Greek-speaking populations
split_reason: Gradually absorbed by surrounding populations; last confirmed speakers recorded in the 1560s.
interesting_fact: We know Crimean Gothic only from a word list of 86 words recorded by Flemish diplomat Ogier Ghiselin de Busbecq in 1569. It is the only evidence the Gothic branch survived beyond the 7th century.

speech_bubbles.translation: Bread. — one of 86 words recorded before this language vanished forever.

name: Old Norse
region: Scandinavia, Iceland, Faroe Islands, Greenland, Normandy, Russia, parts of Britain
speakers_culture: Norse peoples and Vikings; raiders, traders, and settlers across the North Atlantic and into Russia
split_reason: Geographic isolation of settlements and political separation of Scandinavian kingdoms caused divergence into Icelandic, Faroese, Norwegian, Swedish, and Danish.
interesting_fact: Old Norse speakers reached North America ~500 years before Columbus. The Norse settlement at L'Anse aux Meadows, Newfoundland (~1000 CE) is the earliest confirmed European presence in the Americas.

sample_words.meaning: warrior who fights in a trance-like rage

speech_bubbles.translation: West across the sea — they went and found land no European had seen.
Here lies... — runic memorial formula across Scandinavia.

name: Icelandic

parent_influence.old-norse: direct descent; geographic isolation preserved archaic features

speakers_culture: Icelanders — ~370,000 speakers
interesting_fact: Modern Icelanders can read 800-year-old sagas in the original with minimal difficulty. No living language has changed so little in 800 years.

speech_bubbles.translation: Iceland is a beautiful country.
We can read the old sagas — and we do.

name: Faroese
speakers_culture: Faroese people — ~66,000 speakers
interesting_fact: Faroese was banned from official use by Denmark for centuries and had no standardised written form until 1890. The first Faroese Bible wasn't published until 1961.

speech_bubbles.translation: We live in the sea — surrounded by ocean in every direction.

name: Norwegian (Bokmål)

parent_influence.old-danish: Bokmål derives from Danish-influenced written Norwegian during the Danish union

speakers_culture: Norwegians — ~5 million speakers; used by ~85% of the population
interesting_fact: Norway has two official written standards: Bokmål and Nynorsk. Schoolchildren must learn both. They are mutually intelligible.

speech_bubbles.translation: I am Norwegian.

name: Norwegian (Nynorsk)

parent_influence.old-norse: direct descent via rural Norwegian dialects; constructed to be closer to Old Norse than Danish-influenced Bokmål

speakers_culture: Norwegians in western and rural areas — ~600,000 primary users
interesting_fact: Nynorsk was constructed in the 1850s by linguist Ivar Aasen, who travelled Norway collecting rural dialects to create a written standard free from Danish influence.

speech_bubbles.translation: I write Nynorsk — a deliberate choice, not just a dialect.

name: Swedish
speakers_culture: Swedes — ~10 million native speakers
interesting_fact: Swedish uses pitch accent — the same word with different tones means different things. 'Anden' with one tone means 'the duck'; with another it means 'the spirit'.

speech_bubbles.translation: The right amount is best — Swedish cultural philosophy in three words.

name: Danish
speakers_culture: Danes — ~6 million native speakers
interesting_fact: Danish has the 'stød' — a creaky glottal constriction unique among Scandinavian languages. It's one reason Danes are harder for other Scandinavians to understand than Norwegians and Swedes are to each other.

sample_words.meaning: red porridge with cream — notoriously hard to pronounce

speech_bubbles.translation: It's cosy here — Danish at its most cultural.

name: Norn

parent_influence.old-norse: direct descent via West Norse; heavily influenced by Scots before extinction

speakers_culture: Norse settlers of Orkney and Shetland; gradually replaced by Scots
split_reason: Orkney and Shetland were pledged to Scotland by Norway in 1468 as a dowry. Scots gradually replaced Norn over the following centuries.
interesting_fact: Some Shetland fishing terminology preserves Norn words to this day. The last fluent speakers died in the 18th century, but fragments were still being recorded in the 1890s.

sample_words.meaning: sea inlet (survives as 'voe' in Shetland place names)

speech_bubbles.original: Da laek a litel quhil be da burness sied...
speech_bubbles.romanisation: I played a little while by the brook side...
speech_bubbles.translation: Fragment of the last recorded Norn poem, collected 1774.
speech_bubbles.note: attested — one of the last Norn texts

name: Elfdalian

parent_influence.old-norse: direct descent; isolated in Älvdalen valley, retained archaic features lost elsewhere in Scandinavia

speakers_culture: ~2,500 speakers in the Älvdalen valley
interesting_fact: Elfdalian preserves nasal vowels and tonal distinctions lost from Swedish centuries ago. Linguists classify it as a separate language, not a Swedish dialect.

speech_bubbles.translation: I speak Elfdalian — one of the most archaic living Germanic languages.

name: Old Saxon
speakers_culture: Saxon peoples of northwestern Germany
split_reason: Evolved into Middle Low German and eventually Low German / Plattdüütsch.
interesting_fact: The Heliand ('The Saviour'), an Old Saxon epic retelling of the Gospels with Jesus as a Germanic warrior chieftain, is one of the most remarkable cultural translations in medieval literature.

speech_bubbles.translation: Lord, I say to you — Old Saxon Heliand, ~830 CE.

name: Middle Low German
speakers_culture: Hanseatic League merchants; the commercial lingua franca of northern Europe
split_reason: Declined as High German spread northward after Luther's Bible. Evolved into Low German / Plattdüütsch.
interesting_fact: Middle Low German was the trade language of the Hanseatic League. It left vast vocabulary traces in Scandinavian, Estonian, Finnish, Latvian, and Lithuanian through medieval trade contact.

speech_bubbles.translation: The merchant comes from Lübeck — heart of the Hanseatic League.

name: Low German
speakers_culture: ~2–5 million speakers, mostly older; endangered but experiencing cultural revival
interesting_fact: Low German never underwent the High German consonant shift — so 'Water' in Low German is 'Water', not 'Wasser'. Its words often look more like English than German.

sample_words.meaning: water (same as English; High German has Wasser)

speech_bubbles.translation: Hello, hello! — the greeting of the North Sea coast.

name: Old High German
speakers_culture: Franks, Alemanni, Bavarians, Lombards in the southern Germanic regions
split_reason: Evolved into Middle High German ~1050 CE; the High German consonant shift distinguished it permanently from Low German dialects.
interesting_fact: The High German consonant shift is why English has 'make' but German has 'machen', English 'water' but German 'Wasser'. Same PIE root, different evolution.

speech_bubbles.translation: I am your father — Old High German, ~800 CE.

name: Middle High German
speakers_culture: Medieval German-speaking peoples; the Hohenstaufen court made it the prestige literary language
split_reason: Evolved into Early New High German and then Modern German; Yiddish and Luxembourgish also branched from Middle High German.
interesting_fact: The Nibelungenlied — the German equivalent of the Iliad — was written in Middle High German around 1200 CE. It forms the basis of Wagner's Ring Cycle.

speech_bubbles.original: Uns ist in alten mæren wunders vil geseit
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Uns ist in alten mæren wunders vil geseit
speech_bubbles.translation: In old tales many marvels are told to us — opening of the Nibelungenlied, ~1200 CE.

name: German

parent_influence.middle-high-german: direct descent via Early New High German; Luther's Bible translation (~1534) standardised the language

speakers_culture: ~95 million native speakers
interesting_fact: German capitalises all nouns — every single one. 'Der Hund läuft' (the Dog runs). This is unique among major European languages and dates to the 17th century.

sample_words.meaning: pleasure from others' misfortune (loaned into English)
Danube steamship company captain — German compounds have no limit

speech_bubbles.translation: In the beginning was the Word — Luther's Bible, 1534.

name: Swiss German

parent_influence.middle-high-german: direct descent via Alemannic dialects; Alpine isolation preserved archaic features

speakers_culture: ~5 million speakers in Switzerland
interesting_fact: Swiss German is spoken but almost never written — Standard German is used for writing. WhatsApp broke this norm; Swiss German texting is now common.

sample_words.meaning: kitchen cupboard — the famous Swiss German tongue-twister

speech_bubbles.translation: Hello everyone! — the quintessential Swiss German greeting.

name: Luxembourgish

parent_influence.middle-high-german: direct descent via Moselle Franconian dialects

speakers_culture: ~400,000 speakers; one of three official languages of Luxembourg alongside French and German
interesting_fact: Most Luxembourgers are trilingual by default: Luxembourgish at home, French at work, German for reading. Luxembourgish only became an official written language in 1984.

speech_bubbles.original: Mir wëlle bleiwe wat mir sinn.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Mir wëlle bleiwe wat mir sinn.
speech_bubbles.translation: We want to remain what we are — Luxembourg national motto.

name: Pennsylvania Dutch

parent_influence.middle-high-german: primary base via Palatinate German dialects
parent_influence.modern-english: significant English influence over 300 years of contact

region: Pennsylvania, Ohio, Indiana and other US states
speakers_culture: ~300,000 speakers, primarily Amish and Old Order Mennonite communities
interesting_fact: 'Dutch' here is a corruption of 'Deutsch' (German). Pennsylvania Dutch speakers are descended from 18th-century German immigrants, not Dutch people. Their language has evolved in American isolation since the 1700s.

speech_bubbles.translation: We are German people — spoken in Pennsylvania for 300 years.

name: Yiddish

parent_influence.hebrew-aramaic: religious and scholarly vocabulary; written right-to-left in Hebrew script

region: Originated in Rhineland Germany; spread across Eastern Europe; now globally dispersed
speakers_culture: Ashkenazi Jewish communities; ~1.5 million speakers today (reduced from ~11 million pre-Holocaust)
interesting_fact: Before WWII, Yiddish had ~11 million speakers. The Holocaust destroyed most of its speaker communities. Many Yiddish words entered English through New York: chutzpah, schlep, schmooze, bagel.

speech_bubbles.original: אַ מענטש טאָר נישט זיין ווי אַ פֿליג
speech_bubbles.romanisation: A mentsh tor nisht zayn vi a flig
speech_bubbles.translation: A person must not be like a fly — Yiddish proverb.
Peace be upon you — traditional Jewish greeting.

name: Old Frisian

parent_influence.proto-west-germanic: direct descent; Frisian and English share the Ingvaeonic sub-branch

speakers_culture: Frisian coastal peoples
split_reason: Evolved into West Frisian, North Frisian, and Saterland Frisian — now three separate languages.
interesting_fact: Old Frisian and Old English are the closest Old Germanic languages to each other. 'Bread, butter and green cheese is good English and good Frise' — a medieval rhyme highlighting their similarity.

speech_bubbles.translation: All Frisians are free — medieval Frisian legal principle.

name: West Frisian
speakers_culture: ~470,000 speakers; co-official with Dutch in Friesland
interesting_fact: West Frisian is the closest living language to English. 'Good butter and good cheese is good English and good Frise' — a medieval rhyme that still almost works today.

sample_words.meaning: butter, bread and green cheese — the famous Frisian shibboleth

speech_bubbles.original: Bûter, brea en griene tsiis; wa't dat net sizze kin, is gjin oprjochte Fries.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Bûter, brea en griene tsiis; wa't dat net sizze kin, is gjin oprjochte Fries.
speech_bubbles.translation: Butter, bread and green cheese; who cannot say that is no true Frisian.

name: North Frisian
region: Schleswig-Holstein, Germany (North Frisian Islands and coast)
speakers_culture: ~8,000–10,000 speakers; severely endangered
interesting_fact: North Frisian is actually a cluster of ten distinct dialects, some mutually unintelligible. The language is critically endangered.

sample_words.meaning: hello (also used in Low German across northern Germany)

speech_bubbles.translation: Hello! — one of the most recognisable words from the North Sea coast.

name: Saterland Frisian

parent_influence.old-frisian: direct descent; isolated in the Saterland marsh

speakers_culture: ~1,000 speakers; critically endangered
interesting_fact: Fewer than 1,000 speakers remain in a small marshy area of Lower Saxony. It is the most archaic of the three living Frisian languages.

speech_bubbles.translation: That is our language — one of the smallest living Germanic languages.

name: Middle Dutch
speakers_culture: Dutch and Flemish peoples of the medieval Low Countries
split_reason: Evolved into Modern Dutch; Afrikaans later branched from 17th-century Dutch.
interesting_fact: Middle Dutch produced Reynard the Fox, a satirical animal epic translated across Europe. The genre of the 'beast fable' owes much to this tradition.

speech_bubbles.translation: Reynard the Fox — the great Middle Dutch satirical epic, ~1250 CE.

name: Dutch

parent_influence.middle-dutch: direct descent; standardised around the Holland dialect in the 16th–17th century

speakers_culture: ~24 million native speakers
interesting_fact: Dutch gave English more words than most people realise: 'cookie', 'yankee', 'boss', 'cruise', 'landscape', 'mannequin', 'Santa Claus' — all from Dutch.

speech_bubbles.original: In het begin schiep God de hemel en de aarde.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: In het begin schiep God de hemel en de aarde.
speech_bubbles.translation: In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth — Dutch Bible, 1637.

name: Afrikaans

parent_influence.malay-portuguese-creole: Cape Malay vocabulary and simplifications from contact

speakers_culture: ~7 million native speakers; Afrikaners and Cape Coloured communities in South Africa
interesting_fact: Afrikaans is the youngest national language with its own standardised written form. It evolved in the Cape Colony from the 1650s as Dutch settlers, Malay workers, Khoikhoi peoples, and German immigrants communicated.

speech_bubbles.translation: I am an Afrikaner — a language born of the Cape.

name: Scots

parent_influence.old-norse: significant Norse vocabulary from Scandinavian settlement

region: Scotland (especially Lowlands) and parts of Northern Ireland (Ulster-Scots)
speakers_culture: ~1.5 million speakers in Scotland; Ulster-Scots in Northern Ireland
interesting_fact: Robert Burns wrote in Scots — 'Auld Lang Syne' is a Scots poem. Scots is recognised as a regional language under the European Charter but its status as language vs dialect remains politically contested.

speech_bubbles.translation: Should old acquaintance be forgotten — Robert Burns, Auld Lang Syne, 1788.

name: Oscan

parent_influence.proto-italic: direct descent; sister language to Latin, not descended from it

speakers_culture: Samnites and other Oscan-speaking peoples; fierce resisters of Roman expansion
split_reason: Gradually replaced by Latin after Roman conquest; extinct by ~100 CE.
interesting_fact: Oscan was the language of the Samnites, who nearly defeated Rome in the Samnite Wars (343–290 BCE). Had Rome lost, Oscan rather than Latin might be the ancestor of today's Romance languages.

speech_bubbles.translation: He built among the people — Oscan votive inscription.

name: Umbrian

parent_influence.proto-italic: direct descent; Sabellic branch alongside Oscan

speakers_culture: Umbrian peoples of central Italy
split_reason: Replaced by Latin after Roman incorporation of Umbria.
interesting_fact: The Iguvine Tablets — seven bronze tablets found in Gubbio — are the most extensive Umbrian text we have. They describe ritual procedures for purifying the city of Iguvium.

speech_bubbles.translation: To Fisus Sancius, to Fisus Sancius — ritual address from the Iguvine Tablets.

name: Faliscan

parent_influence.proto-italic: direct descent; most closely related to Latin of all Italic languages

region: Southern Etruria (around Falerii, near modern Civita Castellana)
speakers_culture: Faliscans — a small people closely related culturally to the Etruscans but linguistically to the Latins
split_reason: Falerii was destroyed and its population relocated by Rome in 241 BCE; the language quickly became extinct.
interesting_fact: Faliscan is so close to Latin that some scholars classify it as a Latin dialect rather than a separate language. Its inscriptions show gradual assimilation to Latin spelling conventions.

speech_bubbles.translation: Today I shall drink wine; tomorrow I shall go without — earliest Faliscan inscription, ~600 BCE.

name: Old French
speakers_culture: Northern French peoples; Frankish ruling class who had adopted Romance speech
split_reason: Evolved into Middle French ~1300 CE. Regional dialects (Picard, Walloon, Norman) diverged separately.
interesting_fact: La Chanson de Roland (~1100 CE) — the oldest major work of French literature — is an Old French epic about Charlemagne. It was composed while Norman French was being spoken in England.

speech_bubbles.translation: God help us! — battle cry, La Chanson de Roland.

name: Middle French
speakers_culture: French people during the late medieval and early Renaissance period
split_reason: Evolved into Modern French; the printing press and royal standardisation around the Parisian dialect fixed the literary form.
interesting_fact: Rabelais wrote Gargantua and Pantagruel in Middle French (~1530). His prose was so inventive that 'rabelaisian' entered English as an adjective meaning bawdy and satirical.

speech_bubbles.translation: I think, therefore I am — Descartes, 1637.

name: French

parent_influence.middle-french: direct descent; Académie française standardised from 1635

region: France, Belgium, Switzerland, Canada, and 28 countries in Africa
speakers_culture: ~80 million native speakers; ~275 million total
interesting_fact: French was the international language of diplomacy from the 17th century until the mid-20th century. All treaties were written in French. The word 'diplomacy' itself is borrowed from French.

sample_words.meaning: liberty, equality, fraternity — the French Republic's motto
already seen (loaned into English and dozens of other languages)

speech_bubbles.translation: Liberty, equality, fraternity — the three words of the French Republic.

name: Occitan
region: Southern France (Occitania), parts of Italy (Val d'Aosta, Piedmont) and Spain (Val d'Aran)
speakers_culture: ~100,000–800,000 speakers (estimates vary widely); endangered
interesting_fact: The troubadour tradition — the origin of lyric poetry in Western Europe — was written in Old Occitan. Dante considered writing the Divine Comedy in Occitan before choosing Italian.

speech_bubbles.original: Ab la dolchor del temps novel
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Ab la dolchor del temps novel
speech_bubbles.translation: With the sweetness of the new season — Guillaume IX, first known troubadour, ~1100 CE.

name: Franco-Provençal

parent_influence.vulgar-latin: direct descent; distinct Gallo-Romance branch between French and Occitan

speakers_culture: ~100,000 speakers; endangered
interesting_fact: Franco-Provençal is classified as a separate Romance language, not a dialect of French or Occitan, though it was not recognised as such until 1873. Many speakers don't know the language has a name.

speech_bubbles.translation: The weather is fine — everyday Arpitan.

name: Old Spanish
speakers_culture: Castilians of the medieval Iberian Peninsula; expanding during the Reconquista
split_reason: Standardised into Modern Spanish after the unification of Spain (1492) and publication of the first Spanish grammar (also 1492, same year as Columbus).
interesting_fact: The Cantar de Mio Cid (~1200 CE) is the oldest preserved Old Spanish literary text — an epic about the legendary warrior El Cid.

speech_bubbles.original: De los sos ojos tan fuerte miente lorando
speech_bubbles.romanisation: De los sos ojos tan fuerte miente lorando
speech_bubbles.translation: From his eyes, weeping so strongly — Cantar de Mio Cid, ~1200 CE.

name: Spanish
region: Spain, Latin America, United States, Equatorial Guinea
speakers_culture: ~485 million native speakers — second most spoken language by native speakers globally
interesting_fact: Spanish and Portuguese are so mutually intelligible that speakers often communicate in their own language and understand the other — a phenomenon called 'portuñol' at the border.

speech_bubbles.original: En un lugar de la Mancha...
speech_bubbles.romanisation: En un lugar de la Mancha...
speech_bubbles.translation: In a place in La Mancha... — Don Quixote, Cervantes, 1605. The first modern novel.

name: Ladino (Judeo-Spanish)

parent_influence.old-spanish: grammatical base and core vocabulary; preserves 15th-century Spanish forms
parent_influence.hebrew-aramaic: religious vocabulary and some grammatical features; written in Hebrew script (Rashi)

region: Originated in Spain; diaspora spread to Ottoman Empire, North Africa, Balkans; now mainly Israel, Turkey, USA
speakers_culture: Sephardic Jewish communities; ~60,000–150,000 speakers today
interesting_fact: Ladino preserves Old Spanish features lost from Modern Spanish — including sounds that existed in 15th-century Castilian but later shifted. Ladino speakers carried a 1492 time capsule of Spanish across the world.

speech_bubbles.translation: Good luck! — a Ladino blessing mixing Spanish and Hebrew.

name: Catalan

parent_influence.vulgar-latin: direct descent; Ibero-Romance with strong Gallo-Romance features

region: Catalonia, Valencia, Balearic Islands (Spain), Andorra, parts of France (Roussillon)
speakers_culture: ~10 million speakers; official in Andorra; co-official in Catalonia, Valencia, Balearic Islands
interesting_fact: Catalan is not a dialect of Spanish — it is a separate Romance language, closer in structure to Occitan and French than to Castilian. It was banned under Franco's dictatorship (1939–1975).

sample_words.meaning: common sense, wisdom (key Catalan cultural value)

speech_bubbles.translation: Catalonia is not Spain — a statement that has defined Catalan identity for centuries.

name: Old Portuguese

parent_influence.vulgar-latin: direct descent via Ibero-Romance; Galician-Portuguese was originally one language

region: Northwestern Iberian Peninsula (Galicia and northern Portugal)
speakers_culture: Galicians and Portuguese in the medieval Kingdom of Portugal
split_reason: Political separation of Portugal from Galicia caused the common Galician-Portuguese to diverge into two distinct languages.
interesting_fact: Old Portuguese (Galician-Portuguese) was the prestige lyric poetry language of the entire Iberian Peninsula in the 13th century — even Castilian kings wrote poetry in it.

speech_bubbles.translation: Waves of the sea at Vigo — opening of a famous Galician-Portuguese cantiga, ~1200 CE.

name: Portuguese
region: Portugal, Brazil, Angola, Mozambique, Cape Verde, and globally
speakers_culture: ~230 million native speakers — sixth most spoken language by native speakers globally
interesting_fact: Portuguese is the most widely spoken language in the Southern Hemisphere. Brazil alone has ~215 million speakers — more than all other Portuguese-speaking countries combined.

speech_bubbles.original: Em português, a saudade é uma palavra que não tem tradução.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Em português, a saudade é uma palavra que não tem tradução.
speech_bubbles.translation: In Portuguese, saudade is a word without translation — and they are proud of it.

name: Galician

parent_influence.old-portuguese: direct descent; split from Portuguese when Galicia remained part of Spain

speakers_culture: ~2.4 million speakers; co-official in Galicia alongside Spanish
interesting_fact: Galician and Portuguese are so closely related that many linguists regard them as dialects of one language. A Galician speaker and a Portuguese speaker can communicate with relative ease.

speech_bubbles.original: Galicia ten un ceo tan gris que faiña chorar.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Galicia ten un ceo tan gris que faiña chorar.
speech_bubbles.translation: Galicia has such a grey sky that it makes you cry — and yet the food is extraordinary.

name: Mozarabic

parent_influence.arabic: heavy Arabic influence on vocabulary during Islamic rule of Iberia

region: Al-Andalus (Islamic Iberia — modern southern and central Spain)
speakers_culture: Mozarabs — Christians living under Islamic rule in medieval Spain
split_reason: Gradually absorbed by Castilian Spanish and Portuguese as the Reconquista pushed south; extinct by ~1300 CE.
interesting_fact: Mozarabic is known almost entirely from kharjas — short lyric verses appended to Arabic and Hebrew poems. They are the earliest written examples of any Romance language from Iberia.

speech_bubbles.original: Mio sidi Ibrahim, ya nuemne dolche!
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Mio sidi Ibrahim, ya nuemne dolche!
speech_bubbles.translation: My lord Ibrahim, oh sweet name! — kharja, ~11th century CE.

name: Sardinian

parent_influence.vulgar-latin: direct descent; first Romance language to diverge, preserving the most archaic Latin features

speakers_culture: ~1–1.3 million speakers; UNESCO-classified as endangered
interesting_fact: Sardinian is the most conservative Romance language — it diverged first and changed least. It preserves Latin 'domus' (house) instead of 'casa', and 'cantu' instead of 'canto'.

speech_bubbles.translation: God save you, Mary — Sardinian prayer, almost Latin in feel.

name: Old Italian
speakers_culture: Inhabitants of the medieval Italian city-states
split_reason: Evolved into Modern Italian; regional dialects (Venetian, Neapolitan, Sicilian) diverged into separate languages.
interesting_fact: The earliest Italian text is the Placiti Cassinesi (~960 CE): legal documents where witnesses testified in the vernacular because local judges no longer understood Latin.

sample_words.meaning: I know that those lands — opening of the earliest Italian sentence

speech_bubbles.original: Nel mezzo del cammin di nostra vita
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Nel mezzo del cammin di nostra vita
speech_bubbles.translation: Midway through the journey of our life — Dante, Divine Comedy, 1320 CE.

name: Italian

parent_influence.old-italian: direct descent; standardised around Florentine Tuscan dialect via Dante, Petrarch, and Boccaccio

region: Italy, San Marino, Vatican City, parts of Switzerland
speakers_culture: ~65 million native speakers
interesting_fact: Italian is the closest living language to Latin. It also has an unusually high number of words ending in vowels — a feature inherited directly from Latin, which most other Romance languages lost.

sample_words.meaning: hello/goodbye (from Venetian 'schiavo', literally 'I am your slave')
the world's most exported Italian word

speech_bubbles.original: Nel mezzo del cammin di nostra vita mi ritrovai per una selva oscura
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Nel mezzo del cammin di nostra vita mi ritrovai per una selva oscura
speech_bubbles.translation: Midway through the journey of our life I found myself in a dark wood — Dante, Divine Comedy, 1320 CE.

name: Neapolitan

parent_influence.vulgar-latin: direct descent via Italo-Romance; developed separately from Florentine Italian

region: Southern Italy (Naples, Campania, parts of Lazio, Abruzzo, Molise, Basilicata, Calabria)
speakers_culture: ~5.7 million speakers
interesting_fact: Neapolitan is not a dialect of Italian — it is a separate language with its own literary tradition. 'O sole mio' is a Neapolitan song, not Italian.

speech_bubbles.translation: My sun is right in front of you! — 'O sole mio, 1898.

name: Sicilian

parent_influence.arabic: significant vocabulary from Arab rule of Sicily (827–1072 CE)
parent_influence.ancient-greek: substrate from Greek colonisation of Sicily (Magna Graecia)

speakers_culture: ~4.7 million speakers
interesting_fact: The first school of Italian poetry — the Sicilian School, founded under Frederick II in the 13th century — wrote in Sicilian. Dante and Petrarch drew from it to create 'Italian' poetry.

speech_bubbles.translation: Love, love, love! — Sicilian folk song. The vowel shift from 'amore' marks Sicilian as distinct.

name: Venetian

parent_influence.vulgar-latin: direct descent via Italo-Romance; developed under the maritime Republic of Venice

region: Veneto, Friuli-Venezia Giulia (Italy), parts of Croatia, Brazil (Talian dialect)
speakers_culture: ~3.8 million speakers
interesting_fact: Venetian was the official language of the Republic of Venice — one of history's most powerful trading states — and served as a trade lingua franca across the eastern Mediterranean. Marco Polo spoke Venetian.

sample_words.meaning: what's your name? (cf. Italian 'come si chiama?')

speech_bubbles.translation: Long live Saint Mark! — the rallying cry of the Venetian Republic.

name: Dalmatian

parent_influence.vulgar-latin: direct descent via Italo-Romance on the Dalmatian coast

speakers_culture: Inhabitants of the Dalmatian coast under various rulers (Byzantine, Croatian, Venetian, Habsburg)
split_reason: Gradually replaced by Croatian and Venetian; the last native speaker, Antonio Udina, died in an explosion in 1898.
interesting_fact: Dalmatian is known from one man: Antonio Udina, whose speech was recorded by linguist Antonio Ive in the 1870s. He died in 1898 in a gunpowder accident, taking the last living knowledge of Dalmatian with him.

speech_bubbles.translation: Two eyes — recorded from Antonio Udina in the 1870s. One of the last Dalmatian sentences ever spoken.

name: Romanian

parent_influence.vulgar-latin: direct descent via Daco-Romance; isolated from Western Romance by Slavic migrations

region: Romania, Moldova, parts of Ukraine, Serbia, Bulgaria
speakers_culture: ~24 million native speakers
interesting_fact: Romanian is the only major Romance language in Eastern Europe — an island of Latin in a sea of Slavic languages. It preserved Latin features lost even in Italian, including three grammatical genders and a vocative case.

speech_bubbles.translation: Write to me, brother Ioan... — the Neacșu Letter, 1521. The oldest surviving Romanian text.

name: Aromanian

parent_influence.vulgar-latin: direct descent via Eastern Romance; sister language to Romanian, not descended from it

region: Greece, Albania, North Macedonia, Bulgaria, Serbia
speakers_culture: ~300,000 speakers; sometimes called Vlachs; historically nomadic shepherds
interesting_fact: Aromanian speakers (Vlachs) are scattered across the Balkans with no homeland state. Their ancestors were Romanised people who retreated into the mountains as Slavic migrations swept through the region.

speech_bubbles.translation: We are Aromanians — scattered across six countries, speaking Latin's most isolated descendant.

name: Romansh

parent_influence.vulgar-latin: direct descent via Rhaeto-Romance; isolated in Alpine valleys

speakers_culture: ~60,000 speakers; fourth national language of Switzerland
interesting_fact: Romansh is not one language but five distinct written variants plus an artificial unified standard 'Rumantsch Grischun' created in 1982. Many Romansh speakers resent the artificial standard.

speech_bubbles.translation: We read in Romansh — still, after centuries of German pressure.

name: Proto-Balto-Slavic
region: Eastern Europe (modern Poland, Ukraine, Belarus, Baltic states)
speakers_culture: Proto-Balto-Slavic peoples of Eastern Europe
split_reason: Diverged into Baltic and Slavic branches; the split is placed around 500–800 BCE.
interesting_fact: Lithuanian is often cited as the most archaic living IE language — it preserves features of Proto-Balto-Slavic (and even PIE) that were lost in virtually all other IE branches.

name: Proto-Slavic
speakers_culture: Early Slavic peoples before their great migration across Eastern Europe in the 5th–6th centuries CE
split_reason: The Slavic migrations of the 5th–7th centuries CE spread speakers across a vast territory, causing rapid divergence into East, West, and South Slavic branches.
interesting_fact: The Slavic expansion in the 6th century was one of history's great demographic events — from a small heartland in modern Poland/Ukraine, Slavic-speaking peoples spread to dominate half of Europe within 200 years.

name: Old Church Slavonic

parent_influence.proto-slavic: direct descent; based on the South Slavic dialect of Thessaloniki

region: Moravia (modern Czech Republic), then Bulgaria, Serbia, Russia — used across all Slavic Orthodox churches
speakers_culture: Created by Saints Cyril and Methodius for the Christianisation of Slavic peoples; used as a liturgical language across Eastern Orthodox Slavic churches for over a millennium
split_reason: Never a spoken vernacular; remained as a literary and liturgical language while vernacular Slavic languages diverged. Still used in Eastern Orthodox liturgy today.
interesting_fact: Saints Cyril and Methodius invented the Glagolitic alphabet specifically to write Old Church Slavonic, and their students later created the Cyrillic alphabet. Every Slavic language that uses Cyrillic script owes that alphabet to this missionary project.

speech_bubbles.translation: Seek the Lord, and he will enlighten you — Old Church Slavonic psalm.

name: Old East Slavic
speakers_culture: The Kievan Rus state; Varangian (Norse) ruling class who assimilated into Slavic culture
split_reason: The Mongol invasion (1237–1242) shattered the Kievan Rus political unity. Northern principalities (Moscow) and southern/western regions (Ukraine, Belarus) developed separately, eventually producing Russian, Ukrainian, and Belarusian.
interesting_fact: The Primary Chronicle (1113 CE) — the oldest major historical text of the Eastern Slavs — is written in Old East Slavic. It describes the founding of the Kievan Rus and the Varangian origins of the ruling dynasty.

speech_bubbles.translation: These are the tales of bygone years — opening of the Primary Chronicle, 1113 CE.

name: Russian

parent_influence.old-east-slavic: direct descent via the northeastern Russian principalities, especially Moscow

region: Russia and former Soviet states; most widely spoken Slavic language
speakers_culture: ~150 million native speakers; official or widely used in 12 former Soviet republics
interesting_fact: Russian is one of the six official languages of the United Nations. It was the language of the first human in space — Yuri Gagarin's 'Поехали!' ('Let's go!') in 1961 is one of the most famous utterances in any language.

sample_words.meaning: Let's go! — Yuri Gagarin's first word in space
nothing / never mind / it's fine (famously versatile)

speech_bubbles.translation: Let's go! — Yuri Gagarin, April 12, 1961. The first word spoken in space.

name: Ukrainian

parent_influence.old-east-slavic: direct descent via the southwestern dialects

speakers_culture: ~35–40 million native speakers; official language of Ukraine
interesting_fact: Ukrainian was banned from official use in the Russian Empire in 1863 (Ems Decree) and repeatedly suppressed during the Soviet era. Despite this, it survived and is now experiencing a major revival following Russia's 2022 invasion.

speech_bubbles.translation: Glory to Ukraine! — a language that refused to disappear.

name: Belarusian
speakers_culture: ~7–9 million native speakers; one of two official languages of Belarus (alongside Russian)
interesting_fact: Belarusian is an official language of its own country but Russian is more widely used in everyday life. Many Belarusians speak a mixed register called 'Trasyanka'.

speech_bubbles.translation: I have a pure and steadfast faith — Belarusian proverb.

name: Rusyn

parent_influence.old-east-slavic: direct descent; developed in the Carpathian region, sometimes classified as a Ukrainian dialect

speakers_culture: ~600,000 speakers; officially recognised as a minority language in several countries
interesting_fact: Andy Warhol's parents were Rusyn immigrants from the village of Mikorov in modern Slovakia. The pop art icon was of Rusyn heritage.

speech_bubbles.translation: We are Rusyns. We are here — a language debated, a people persistent.

name: Polish

parent_influence.proto-slavic: direct descent via West Slavic (Lechitic branch)

region: Poland; large diaspora in USA, Germany, UK
speakers_culture: ~45 million native speakers; the largest West Slavic language
interesting_fact: Polish has seven grammatical cases and grammatical gender that affects adjectives, verbs, and even numbers. The number 'two' has five different forms depending on what is being counted.

sample_words.original: W Szczebrzeszynie chrząszcz brzmi w trzcinie
sample_words.romanisation: V Shchebzheshynie khzhonshch bzhmi v tshchinie
sample_words.meaning: In Szczebrzeszyn a beetle buzzes in the reeds — the famous Polish tongue-twister

speech_bubbles.original: Polska nie zginęła, kiedy my żyjemy.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Polska nie zginęła, kiedy my żyjemy.
speech_bubbles.translation: Poland has not perished so long as we still live — opening of the Polish national anthem.

name: Czech

parent_influence.proto-slavic: direct descent via West Slavic (Bohemian branch)

speakers_culture: ~10 million native speakers
interesting_fact: The word 'robot' was coined by Czech writer Karel Čapek in 1920, from Czech 'robota' (forced labour). It is one of the most globally influential words any language has contributed to the world.

sample_words.meaning: robot (coined by Karel Čapek in 1920 — one of Czech's most globally influential words)
beer (Czech produces some of the world's most acclaimed)

speech_bubbles.translation: Truth prevails — Czech national motto, also motto of Jan Hus.

name: Slovak
speakers_culture: ~5.5 million native speakers
interesting_fact: Czech and Slovak are so mutually intelligible that Czechoslovakia functioned without any translation infrastructure. After the peaceful 'Velvet Divorce' in 1993, they remain mutually intelligible.

sample_words.meaning: hello / hi (from English 'ahoy', the sailor's call)

speech_bubbles.translation: Lightning flashes over the Tatra Mountains — opening of the Slovak national anthem.

name: Upper Sorbian

parent_influence.proto-slavic: direct descent via West Slavic (Sorbian branch)

speakers_culture: ~13,000 speakers; one of only two Slavic languages spoken in Germany
interesting_fact: The Sorbs are the only Slavic people with an indigenous homeland entirely within Germany. They have maintained their language and culture for 1,500 years surrounded by German speakers.

speech_bubbles.translation: My mother tongue is Sorbian — a Slavic language in the heart of Germany.

name: Lower Sorbian

parent_influence.proto-slavic: direct descent via West Slavic (Sorbian branch)

speakers_culture: ~7,000 speakers; critically endangered
interesting_fact: Lower Sorbian has fewer than 7,000 speakers and is critically endangered. The last native-speaker generation is elderly; active revitalisation efforts are underway in schools.

speech_bubbles.translation: Our language is still alive.

name: Kashubian

parent_influence.proto-slavic: direct descent via West Slavic (Pomeranian branch); influenced by Polish, German, and Low German

speakers_culture: ~100,000–200,000 speakers; the only regional language with official status in Poland
interesting_fact: Kashubian is the only language other than Polish with official recognition in Poland. It retains features of the extinct Pomeranian branch, making it a linguistic bridge between Polish and the lost Baltic coast languages.

speech_bubbles.original: Nie ma Kaszêb bez Polské, ani Polské bez Kaszêb.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Nie ma Kaszêb bez Polské, ani Polské bez Kaszêb.
speech_bubbles.translation: There is no Kashubia without Poland, and no Poland without Kashubia.

name: Polabian

parent_influence.proto-slavic: direct descent via West Slavic (Pomeranian/Lechitic branch)

region: Elbe River region, northern Germany (modern Saxony-Anhalt, Brandenburg)
speakers_culture: Polabian Slavs of the Elbe region; gradually Germanised over centuries
split_reason: Replaced by German through assimilation over several centuries. The last speakers died around 1750 CE.
interesting_fact: The name 'Berlin' derives from a Polabian word meaning 'swamp'. The language died around 1750, documented only by German scholars who recorded word lists from the last speakers.

speech_bubbles.translation: The night falls — recorded in the last Polabian wordlists, ~1700 CE.

name: Serbian

parent_influence.proto-slavic: direct descent via South Slavic; standardised by Vuk Stefanović Karadžić in the 1810s–1820s

speakers_culture: ~10 million native speakers
interesting_fact: Serbian is one of the rare languages written in two scripts — Cyrillic (official) and Latin (widely used). A Serbian speaker can fluidly switch between both, and most publications appear in both.

speech_bubbles.original: Србија је земља истока и запада.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Srbija je zemlja istoka i zapada.
speech_bubbles.translation: Serbia is a land of east and west.

name: Croatian

parent_influence.proto-slavic: direct descent via South Slavic; shares common base with Serbian but has a distinct literary tradition

speakers_culture: ~5–6 million native speakers
interesting_fact: The necktie was invented in Croatia — Croatian mercenaries in 17th-century France wore distinctive scarves ('cravate', from 'Croat') that the French court adopted as fashion. The word 'cravat' still exists in English.

speech_bubbles.translation: Our beautiful homeland — opening of the Croatian national anthem.

name: Bulgarian

parent_influence.old-church-slavonic: direct descent; Old Church Slavonic was based on the Old Bulgarian dialect of Thessaloniki

speakers_culture: ~8–9 million native speakers
interesting_fact: Bulgarian is the only South Slavic language that has completely lost its case system (along with Macedonian). It uses articles (like English 'the') instead — also unique among Slavic languages.

speech_bubbles.translation: Liberty or death! — Bulgarian revolutionary slogan against Ottoman rule.

name: Slovenian
speakers_culture: ~2.5 million native speakers
interesting_fact: Slovenian is the only Slavic language to retain the dual grammatical number from Proto-Slavic — a special form for exactly two of something. 'Two dogs' uses a different verb form than 'three dogs'.

speech_bubbles.translation: Long live Slovenia!

name: Lithuanian
speakers_culture: ~3 million native speakers
interesting_fact: Lithuanian is the most archaic living IE language — it preserves features of PIE that disappeared from all other branches millennia ago. The Lithuanian word for smoke, 'dumas', is cognate with PIE '*dhuh₂mos' and barely changed in 6,000 years.

speech_bubbles.translation: Lithuania, our homeland — opening of the Lithuanian national anthem.

name: Latvian

parent_influence.proto-balto-slavic: direct descent via Proto-Baltic; sibling of Lithuanian

speakers_culture: ~1.75 million native speakers
interesting_fact: Latvian has a distinct 'broken tone' — a falling-rising pitch contour that does not exist in Lithuanian. Despite being Baltic siblings, the two languages are not mutually intelligible.

speech_bubbles.translation: God bless Latvia! — the Latvian national anthem.

name: Old Prussian

parent_influence.proto-balto-slavic: direct descent via Proto-Baltic; the third Baltic language

region: Prussia (modern Kaliningrad, Russia and northeastern Poland)
speakers_culture: The indigenous Prussian people; conquered by the Teutonic Knights in the 13th century
split_reason: The Teutonic Knights conquered Prussia in the 13th century. German colonisation gradually replaced Prussian. The last known speaker died around 1700.
interesting_fact: The country 'Prussia' that dominated 19th-century European politics had nothing to do with the Prussian language. German colonists kept the name of the land whose language they had extinguished.

speech_bubbles.translation: Health! Health! — the Prussian toast, one of the last recorded Prussian phrases.

name: Latgalian

parent_influence.proto-balto-slavic: direct descent via Proto-Baltic; closely related to Latvian but considered a separate language by some

speakers_culture: ~150,000–200,000 speakers in eastern Latvia; predominantly Roman Catholic (unlike Lutheran western Latvia)
interesting_fact: Latgale was under Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth rule while western Latvia was under Sweden and Germany. This centuries-long political separation allowed Latgalian to develop distinctly from Latvian.

speech_bubbles.translation: I speak Latgalian.

name: Proto-Hellenic
speakers_culture: Pre-Greek Indo-European migrants to the Aegean
split_reason: Diverged into ancient Greek dialects (Mycenaean, Doric, Ionic, Aeolic) following migration into the Greek peninsula.
interesting_fact: The earliest attested Greek writing — Linear B (Mycenaean Greek, ~1400 BCE) — predates the Greek alphabet by 700 years. When Linear B was deciphered in 1952 by Michael Ventris, it revealed a form of Greek far older than Homer.

name: Mycenaean Greek
speakers_culture: Mycenaean palace civilisation — the Bronze Age Greeks who fought at Troy
split_reason: The Bronze Age Collapse (~1200 BCE) destroyed Mycenaean civilisation. Writing disappeared for 400 years (the Greek Dark Ages). When it returned, Linear B had been replaced by the alphabet.
interesting_fact: Mycenaean Greek was written in Linear B — a syllabic script deciphered by architect Michael Ventris in 1952. The tablets are almost entirely administrative records: lists of livestock, grain, and workers. The Trojan War heroes left no personal writings.

sample_words.meaning: Achilles — the name appears on a Linear B tablet

speech_bubbles.translation: To the Lady of the Labyrinth — offering record, Knossos tablet, ~1400 BCE.

name: Ancient Greek

parent_influence.mycenaean-greek: direct descent through the Dark Ages; emerged in new alphabetic form ~800 BCE

region: Greece, Aegean, Asia Minor, Sicily, southern Italy, Black Sea coast
speakers_culture: Classical Greeks; city-state civilisation; the Athens of Socrates, Plato, Sophocles, Thucydides
split_reason: Alexander the Great's conquests spread Greek as a common language (Koine) across the eastern Mediterranean.
interesting_fact: Ancient Greek has more surviving literature than any other ancient language — the complete works of Plato, Aristotle, Homer, Thucydides, Sophocles, and Euripides. Its scientific and philosophical vocabulary still dominates global academia.

sample_words.meaning: I have found it — Archimedes (loaned into English)

speech_bubbles.translation: Know thyself — inscribed at the Temple of Apollo at Delphi.
Tell me of that man, O Muse — Homer, Odyssey, ~800 BCE.

name: Koine Greek

parent_influence.ancient-greek: direct descent; levelled Attic dialect spread by Alexander's conquests

region: Eastern Mediterranean, Egypt, Levant, Anatolia — the Hellenistic world
speakers_culture: The lingua franca of the Hellenistic and Roman eastern Mediterranean; the language of the New Testament
split_reason: Evolved into Byzantine Greek as the Roman Empire's eastern half endured.
interesting_fact: The New Testament was written in Koine Greek — not Classical Greek or Hebrew. Christianity's foundational texts are in the street Greek of a Roman province, not the literary language of Athens.

sample_words.meaning: love (the highest, selfless form — used throughout the New Testament)

speech_bubbles.translation: In the beginning was the Word — Gospel of John, ~90 CE. Written in Koine Greek.

name: Byzantine Greek

parent_influence.koine-greek: direct descent; continued as the official language of the Eastern Roman Empire

region: Byzantine Empire (Eastern Roman Empire) — Constantinople, Anatolia, Greece, Egypt, Levant
speakers_culture: Eastern Romans (Byzantines); the only continuous Roman state to survive antiquity
split_reason: The fall of Constantinople in 1453 CE ended the Byzantine Empire. Byzantine Greek evolved into Modern Greek.
interesting_fact: The Byzantines never called themselves Byzantine — they called themselves Romans ('Rhomaioi') and considered themselves the continuation of the Roman Empire. Their Greek was Roman Greek.

sample_words.meaning: King of Kings — Byzantine imperial title

speech_bubbles.translation: The City has fallen — 1453 CE. The City had only one name that needed no explanation.

name: Modern Greek
speakers_culture: ~13 million native speakers
interesting_fact: Greek has been continuously written for 3,400 years — longer than any other European language. Modern Greeks can read Classical texts with study, and ancient inscriptions can still be parsed by educated speakers.

sample_words.meaning: sense of honour, love of excellence (key Greek cultural concept)

speech_bubbles.translation: Freedom or Death! — motto of the Greek War of Independence, 1821.

name: Tsakonian

parent_influence.ancient-greek: direct descent via Doric Greek; not descended from Koine like Modern Greek

speakers_culture: ~200–1,000 speakers; critically endangered
interesting_fact: Tsakonian is the only surviving descendant of Doric Greek — the dialect of Sparta. It is not mutually intelligible with Modern Greek and represents a separate branch that diverged over 2,000 years ago in the mountains of the Peloponnese.

sample_words.meaning: water (cf. Ancient Greek 'hudor'; Tsakonian preserves Doric forms)

speech_bubbles.translation: We are Tsakonians — speaking Sparta's ancient tongue in a forgotten corner of Greece.

name: Proto-Armenian

parent_influence.proto-indo-european: direct descent; Armenian is an independent branch with no close relatives

speakers_culture: Early Armenian-speaking peoples of the Anatolian highlands
split_reason: Developed into Classical Armenian (Grabar) as the Armenian kingdom consolidated.
interesting_fact: Armenian is an IE branch of one — it has no close living relatives. It borrowed so heavily from Iranian and Greek that scholars initially classified it as Iranian before its independent status was recognised in 1875.

name: Classical Armenian
speakers_culture: The Armenian Apostolic Church and scholarly tradition; still used in liturgy
split_reason: Frozen as a liturgical language; evolved into Eastern and Western Armenian as spoken forms.
interesting_fact: Saint Mesrop Mashtots invented the Armenian alphabet in 405 CE specifically to translate the Bible. It has 38 letters and has remained essentially unchanged for 1,600 years. The first sentence written in it was: 'To know wisdom and instruction.'

sample_words.meaning: To know wisdom — first sentence written in Armenian alphabet, 405 CE

speech_bubbles.translation: To know wisdom and instruction — the first words written in Armenian, 405 CE.

name: Eastern Armenian

parent_influence.classical-armenian: direct descent; developed in the Ararat plain under Russian/Persian rule

speakers_culture: ~5 million native speakers; official language of Armenia
interesting_fact: Eastern and Western Armenian diverged dramatically after the Armenian Genocide (1915–1923), which eliminated most Western Armenian speakers from Anatolia. The two dialects are partially mutually intelligible.

speech_bubbles.translation: Armenia forever — a sentiment carried through genocide and diaspora.

name: Western Armenian

parent_influence.classical-armenian: direct descent; developed in western Anatolia and Constantinople

region: Global diaspora — USA, France, Lebanon, Argentina, Syria
speakers_culture: ~500,000–1.5 million speakers; a diaspora language without a territorial homeland since 1915
interesting_fact: Western Armenian is classified by UNESCO as definitely endangered. It was the dominant language of eastern Anatolia before the Armenian Genocide, which killed 1–1.5 million Armenians. Its speakers now exist only in diaspora communities worldwide.

speech_bubbles.translation: Do not forget — a language carried in memory, not geography.

name: Albanian

parent_influence.proto-indo-european: direct descent via an unknown ancient Balkan language (possibly Illyrian or Thracian); Albanian is an IE branch of one

region: Albania, Kosovo, North Macedonia, southern Serbia, southern Montenegro, southern Italy (Arbëreshë)
speakers_culture: ~7.5 million native speakers
interesting_fact: Albanian's ancestry is completely unclear — it has no confirmed ancestor language. It shows traces of Illyrian, Thracian, and Dacian influence but cannot be definitively linked to any ancient language. It is an IE island with no confirmed relatives.

speech_bubbles.translation: The Albanian keeps his besa — an oath of protection or honour that cannot be broken.
Long live Albania!

name: Proto-Celtic
region: Central Europe (Hallstatt culture, modern Austria/southern Germany) — then spreading across Europe
speakers_culture: Hallstatt and La Tène Iron Age cultures; the earliest Celtic peoples
split_reason: Celtic-speaking peoples spread across Europe from their Central European homeland, diverging into Gaulish (continental), Goidelic (Ireland), and Brythonic (Britain) branches.
interesting_fact: At their peak (~300 BCE), Celtic speakers covered more of Europe than any other language group — from Ireland to Galatia (Turkey). Today, Celtic languages cling to the western Atlantic fringe.

name: Gaulish
region: Gaul (modern France, Belgium, northern Italy, parts of the Balkans)
speakers_culture: Gauls; the dominant culture of pre-Roman Western Europe
split_reason: Roman conquest of Gaul (58–50 BCE) brought Latin as a prestige language. Gaulish gradually died out over the following 400 years, replaced by Vulgar Latin — which became French.
interesting_fact: Julius Caesar's Gallic Wars were fought against Gaulish speakers. Caesar wrote that the Druids memorised vast quantities of religious knowledge — so much that students studied for 20 years. Not a word of Druidic lore survives.

speech_bubbles.translation: In Alise they dedicate — from a Gaulish inscription at Alesia, the site of the last Gaulish stand against Caesar.

name: Celtiberian

parent_influence.proto-celtic: direct descent; Celtic branch that settled in the Iberian Peninsula

region: Iberian plateau (modern Castile, Aragon, parts of Portugal)
speakers_culture: Celtiberian tribes of the central Iberian plateau; fierce warriors who resisted Roman conquest
split_reason: Replaced by Latin following Roman conquest. Numantia (133 BCE), the last great Celtiberian stronghold, destroyed itself rather than surrender.
interesting_fact: The siege of Numantia (134–133 BCE) — the last great Celtiberian stronghold — required Scipio Aemilianus to build a 9km circumvallation wall around the city. When the walls closed, the Numantians burned their city and died rather than surrender.

sample_words.meaning: city name (modern Arcos de Jalón; 'areko' = near, 'rata' = fort)

speech_bubbles.translation: Celtiberian inscription on a bronze tablet — the legal and commercial language of Iron Age Spain.

name: Lepontic

parent_influence.proto-celtic: direct descent; the earliest attested Celtic language

region: Alpine region (modern northern Italy and southern Switzerland, around Lake Como and Lake Maggiore)
speakers_culture: Lepontic Celts of the Alpine lakes region
split_reason: Absorbed by Gaulish as Gallic migrations expanded, then both replaced by Latin under Roman rule.
interesting_fact: Lepontic is the earliest attested Celtic language — inscriptions date to the 6th century BCE. A 1994 inscription reading 'Pelkui Pruiam Teu' ('Pelkos gave Prua to Teu') preserves what appears to be a personal transaction from the Iron Age.

speech_bubbles.translation: Pelkos gave Pruia to Teu — an Alpine Celtic land transaction, 500 BCE.

name: Galatian

parent_influence.gaulish: direct descent; Galatian was Gaulish transported to Anatolia by migrating Celtic tribes

region: Galatia (central Anatolia, modern Turkey — modern provinces around Ankara)
speakers_culture: Celtic tribes (Galatians) who migrated from Europe and settled in Anatolia ~278 BCE
split_reason: Replaced by Greek, which was the dominant language of Anatolia. St Jerome (347–420 CE) wrote that the Galatians still spoke a language similar to the Gauls of Trier in his time.
interesting_fact: St Paul's Epistle to the Galatians was written to Celtic speakers in central Turkey. These were the descendants of European Celts who had migrated 700 years earlier — Celts who had sacked Delphi, terrified Macedonia, and settled in the heart of the Greek world.

sample_words.meaning: a band of men (borrowed into Greek and eventually into English as 'drone')

speech_bubbles.translation: Divine Bull — name of the Galatian king defended by Cicero in 45 BCE. A Celtic king, tried in Rome.

name: Old Irish

parent_influence.proto-celtic: direct descent via Primitive Irish (attested in Ogham inscriptions)

speakers_culture: Early medieval Irish monks and nobility; the Irish were the great preservers of classical learning during Europe's Dark Ages
split_reason: Evolved into Middle Irish, which then diverged into Modern Irish, Scottish Gaelic, and Manx.
interesting_fact: Irish monks copied and preserved ancient Greek and Latin manuscripts while the rest of Europe descended into chaos after Rome fell. The Book of Kells (~800 CE), written in Old Irish, is one of the world's most elaborate illuminated manuscripts.

speech_bubbles.original: Is mebul don láich lúsaid a chlaideb día táir.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Is mebul don láich lúsaid a chlaideb día táir.
speech_bubbles.translation: It is disgraceful for a warrior to abandon his sword when he flees — Old Irish, ~800 CE.

name: Middle Irish
speakers_culture: Medieval Irish and Scottish Gaelic speakers before the two dialects became distinct
split_reason: Diverged into Early Modern Irish (parent of Modern Irish and Scottish Gaelic) and Manx.
interesting_fact: The Brian Boru saga and the earliest Finnégacht tales (legends of Fionn Mac Cumhaill — the basis of the Fenian cycle) were written in Middle Irish. These stories would inspire James Macpherson's Ossian poems, which sparked the Romantic movement across Europe.

sample_words.meaning: companion of God (early Irish monasticism concept)

speech_bubbles.original: Is maith an scealuidhe an aimsir.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Is maith an scéalaí an aimsir.
speech_bubbles.translation: Time is a great storyteller — Middle Irish proverb.

name: Irish (Gaeilge)

parent_influence.middle-irish: direct descent via Early Modern Irish

region: Ireland (official language), particularly the Gaeltacht regions of western Ireland
speakers_culture: ~170,000 daily speakers; ~1.8 million with some proficiency; first official language of Ireland
interesting_fact: Irish verbs come first in a sentence — the language is Verb-Subject-Object. This is extremely rare among European languages but common in Irish and the other Celtic languages. 'Is maith liom té' literally means 'Is good with-me tea.'

sample_words.meaning: You are right (literally: 'is the right at-you')
tea (borrowed from English but sounds more archaic)

speech_bubbles.original: Iséard é Gaeilge áille na n-éan ar béal na h-aille.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Iséard é Gaeilge áille na n-éan ar béal na h-aille.
speech_bubbles.translation: The Irish language is the birdsong on the cliff's edge. — A language that almost fell but did not.

name: Scottish Gaelic

parent_influence.middle-irish: direct descent; diverged from Irish after the 12th century

region: Scottish Highlands and Islands (particularly the Outer Hebrides)
speakers_culture: ~57,000 speakers; the language of Highland culture, clans, and the Clearances
interesting_fact: The Highland Clearances (1750–1860) forcibly evicted tens of thousands of Gaelic speakers from the Highlands to make way for sheep farming. This more than any other single event caused the collapse of Gaelic-speaking communities.

sample_words.meaning: Good health! (the Highland toast; 'mhath' pronounced approximately 'va')

speech_bubbles.original: Chan eil an cànan marbh fhathast.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Chan eil an cànan marbh fhathast.
speech_bubbles.translation: The language is not dead yet.

name: Manx

parent_influence.middle-irish: direct descent; diverged from Irish after Norse settlement of the Isle of Man

speakers_culture: ~1,800 speakers; declared 'extinct' in 1974 when the last native speaker died, then revived
interesting_fact: Manx was officially declared extinct when Ned Maddrell — the last native speaker — died in 1974. It was brought back from recordings of native speakers. Today there are Manx-medium schools and ~1,800 speakers. A genuine language resurrection.

speech_bubbles.translation: It is not dead yet — Manx was declared extinct in 1974 and then rose again.

name: Proto-Brythonic

parent_influence.proto-celtic: direct descent; the Celtic branch that settled in Britain

speakers_culture: The ancient Britons; the population of Britain before the Anglo-Saxon invasions
split_reason: The Anglo-Saxon invasions of the 5th century pushed Brythonic speakers west (Wales, Cornwall) and some fled to Brittany (France), creating Welsh, Cornish, and Breton.
interesting_fact: The historical King Arthur — if he existed — was a Proto-Brythonic speaker, a Romano-British leader resisting the Anglo-Saxon invasions. He would have sounded nothing like the medieval French-speaking court of legend.

name: Old Welsh
region: Wales and the Old North (Yr Hen Ogledd — parts of modern northern England and southern Scotland)
speakers_culture: The Welsh people and the Britons of the Old North (Rheged, Gododdin, Strathclyde)
split_reason: Evolved into Middle Welsh. Cumbrian (spoken in the Old North) died out as those kingdoms were absorbed by the Anglo-Saxons and Scots.
interesting_fact: Y Gododdin (~600 CE) — a lament for warriors killed at the Battle of Catraeth — is one of the oldest Welsh poems and contains the first known reference to Arthur: 'he fed black ravens... though he was not Arthur.'

sample_words.meaning: The spotted, arm-bearing creature (from an Old Welsh riddle, ~800 CE)

speech_bubbles.translation: The Gododdin speaks, says Aneirin — opening of Y Gododdin, ~600 CE. The oldest Welsh poetry.

name: Welsh
speakers_culture: ~900,000 speakers; experiencing a significant revival
interesting_fact: Welsh is the oldest continuous literary tradition of any language in Britain. Welsh-medium education has grown dramatically since the 1970s — today ~30% of Welsh children attend Welsh-medium schools.

sample_words.meaning: a longing for Wales, home, or something lost (related to Irish 'deorai', exile)

speech_bubbles.original: Ni waeth dim and o r ddaear godi.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Ni waeth dim and o r ddaear godi.
speech_bubbles.translation: Nothing matters except rising from the earth — Welsh proverb.

name: Breton

parent_influence.proto-brythonic: direct descent; carried to Brittany by British refugees fleeing Anglo-Saxon invasions in the 5th–6th centuries CE

speakers_culture: ~200,000 speakers; severely endangered
interesting_fact: Breton is the only surviving Celtic language on the European continent — and it got there in the 5th–6th centuries when refugees from Britain crossed the Channel to escape the Anglo-Saxon invasions. They named their new home 'Brittany' (Little Britain).

speech_bubbles.original: Ar brezhoneg a zo bev c'hoazh.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Ar brezhoneg a zo bev c'hoazh.
speech_bubbles.translation: Breton is still alive — the only Celtic language in France.

name: Cornish
speakers_culture: ~3,000–4,000 speakers; language revived after last native speaker (Dolly Pentreath) died in 1777
interesting_fact: The last native Cornish speaker, Dolly Pentreath, died in 1777. Like Manx, Cornish was brought back from texts and dictionaries. Today there are Cornish-medium classes and even a small number of children raised with Cornish as a first language.

speech_bubbles.original: An tavas keltek a vew yn-nowydh.
speech_bubbles.romanisation: An tavas keltek a vew yn-nowydh.
speech_bubbles.translation: The Celtic tongue lives anew — Cornish, dead for 200 years, resurrected.

name: Cumbric

parent_influence.proto-brythonic: direct descent; the Brythonic dialect of the Old North kingdoms

region: Cumbria and surrounding areas (northern England and southern Scotland)
speakers_culture: The Brythonic kingdoms of the Old North: Rheged, Strathclyde, Gododdin
split_reason: Absorbed by Old English as the Anglo-Saxon kingdoms expanded northward and the British kingdoms fell. Last attested ~1100 CE.
interesting_fact: Sheep-counting systems in northern England — 'yan, tan, tethera, methera, pip...' — may be remnants of Cumbric. Shepherds in Cumbria, Yorkshire, and other areas used these systems into the 20th century.

speech_bubbles.translation: One, two, three... — the traditional sheep-counting numerals of the English north. Possible Cumbric survival.

name: Proto-Indo-Iranian
region: Central Asian steppes (Andronovo culture region, modern Kazakhstan/Uzbekistan)
speakers_culture: Proto-Indo-Iranian peoples of the Central Asian steppes; semi-nomadic pastoralists
split_reason: The branch split into Iranian (moving west and south into Iran) and Indo-Aryan (moving east and south into the Indian subcontinent through the Hindu Kush).
interesting_fact: Proto-Indo-Iranian speakers are the common ancestors of both the people who wrote the Vedas (the oldest Sanskrit texts) and those who wrote the Avesta (the Zoroastrian scripture). These were sister cultures that diverged but retained remarkable parallels: both have a sacred fire ritual, a divine drink (soma / haoma), and cognate gods.

name: Proto-Iranian
speakers_culture: Proto-Iranian peoples moving onto the Iranian plateau and Central Asia
split_reason: Diverged into Avestan, Old Persian, Scythian/Saka, and the Sogdian/Bactrian branches.
interesting_fact: Proto-Iranian speakers were the founders of one of the great ancient civilisations — the Persian Empire. Their language family today stretches from Kurdistan to Tajikistan to the Pamir Mountains.

name: Avestan
speakers_culture: Zoroastrian religious community; language of Zarathustra (Zoroaster)
split_reason: Never spoken as a vernacular after ~400 BCE; preserved as the liturgical language of Zoroastrianism.
interesting_fact: The Gathas — the oldest hymns of the Avesta — are attributed to Zoroaster himself and may be among the oldest religious texts in any IE language, comparable in age to the Rigveda. The Zoroastrian fire temples maintain rituals in Avestan to this day.

sample_words.meaning: lord (cf. Sanskrit 'asura'; the Vedic and Avestan traditions inverted their valuations of this word)

speech_bubbles.translation: Righteousness is good, it is the best — Zoroastrian prayer, the Ashem Vohu. Recited daily.

name: Old Persian
speakers_culture: Achaemenid Persian Empire; the language of Cyrus the Great, Darius I, and Xerxes
split_reason: Evolved into Middle Persian (Pahlavi) as the Achaemenid Empire gave way to the Parthian and then Sasanian empires.
interesting_fact: The Behistun Inscription (522 BCE) — carved by Darius I on a cliff in Iran — was the Rosetta Stone of Old Persian. Its trilingual text (Old Persian, Elamite, Babylonian) allowed scholars to decipher cuneiform. Henry Rawlinson dangled from a cliff on ropes to copy it in the 1840s.

speech_bubbles.translation: I am Darius the King — Behistun Inscription, 522 BCE.

name: Middle Persian (Pahlavi)
region: Sasanian Persian Empire (modern Iran, Iraq, parts of Central Asia)
speakers_culture: Parthian and Sasanian Persian empires; Zoroastrian religious literature
split_reason: The Arab conquest of Persia (633–651 CE) introduced Arabic. Middle Persian evolved into New Persian (Dari/Farsi), incorporating massive Arabic vocabulary.
interesting_fact: The Shahnameh — Ferdowsi's epic poem (~1000 CE) — was written in New Persian but specifically to revive Persian literary heritage after centuries of Arabic dominance. Ferdowsi famously used minimal Arabic vocabulary as a nationalist statement.

sample_words.meaning: king (loaned into English, Arabic, Turkish, and many other languages)

speech_bubbles.translation: Fire is the good worker — Zoroastrian liturgical Pahlavi.

name: Persian (Farsi)

parent_influence.middle-persian: direct descent; grammatical structure and core vocabulary
parent_influence.arabic: ~50% of vocabulary from Arabic after the Islamic conquest

speakers_culture: ~65–70 million native speakers in Iran
interesting_fact: Persian was the literary and court language of the Mughal Empire in India — a Muslim dynasty that ruled a largely Hindu population, using an Iranian language. Urdu emerged from this Persian-Arabic-Hindi contact.

speech_bubbles.translation: Listen to this reed — opening of Rumi's Masnavi, ~1260 CE. Persian poetry that shaped world literature.

name: Dari (Afghan Persian)
