parent_influence.middle-persian: direct descent; the eastern variety of New Persian

speakers_culture: ~15–20 million speakers in Afghanistan; lingua franca of the country
interesting_fact: Dari preserves many archaic Persian features lost in Iranian Farsi. It is closer to classical Persian poetry than modern colloquial Farsi. Some scholars argue Dari is the direct descendant of the court Persian of the Samanid dynasty.

speech_bubbles.original: آب و آتش دو یار دیرینا
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Āb wa ātash do yār dīrīnā
speech_bubbles.translation: Water and fire, two old friends — Dari proverb from Afghanistan.

name: Tajik

parent_influence.middle-persian: direct descent; the Central Asian variety of New Persian
parent_influence.russian: significant Russian influence during Soviet era; written in Cyrillic since 1939

region: Tajikistan (official); also spoken in Afghanistan and Uzbekistan
speakers_culture: ~8 million speakers; official language of Tajikistan
interesting_fact: Tajik is the only variety of Persian written in Cyrillic script — a legacy of Soviet nationality policy that imposed Cyrillic to separate Tajiks from Persian-speaking Iran. Before 1939, Tajik was written in Arabic script.

speech_bubbles.translation: The Tajik language is alive — Persian, written in Cyrillic, in the Pamirs.

name: Pashto

parent_influence.proto-iranian: direct descent via Eastern Iranian branch

speakers_culture: ~60–70 million speakers (Pashtun people); official language of Afghanistan
interesting_fact: Pashto is an Eastern Iranian language, making it more closely related to Sogdian and Scythian than to Persian. The Pashtunwali code of honour — an ancient system of tribal law — is transmitted entirely through the Pashto language.

speech_bubbles.translation: Pashto is mine, mine — declaration of language pride.

name: Kurdish (Kurmanji)
region: Turkey, Syria, Iraq, Iran (Greater Kurdistan region)
speakers_culture: ~15–20 million speakers of Kurmanji; total Kurdish speakers ~30–40 million (world's largest stateless nation)
interesting_fact: The Kurds are the world's largest ethnic group without their own sovereign state — roughly 30–40 million people split across four countries. Kurdish has been suppressed in Turkey, Syria, Iraq, and Iran at various points in history.

speech_bubbles.translation: Kurdistan is a homeland without borders — a nation in every mountain.

name: Balochi
speakers_culture: ~10 million speakers; Baloch people of Balochistan
interesting_fact: Balochi has a rich oral literary tradition of epic poetry (shā'eri) and has been written in multiple scripts over its history. The largest Balochi-speaking population is in Pakistan's Balochistan, a region roughly the size of France.

speech_bubbles.translation: The Balochi language is free.

name: Ossetian

parent_influence.proto-iranian: direct descent via Scythian/Alanic branch — the descendant of the ancient Scythian and Sarmatian languages of the Eurasian steppe

region: North Ossetia (Russia) and South Ossetia (disputed territory between Russia and Georgia)
speakers_culture: ~600,000 speakers; official language of North Ossetia-Alania (Russia) and South Ossetia
interesting_fact: Ossetian is the last surviving descendant of the Scythian and Sarmatian languages — the nomadic peoples who dominated the Eurasian steppe from Ukraine to Kazakhstan for a thousand years. The Alans, ancestors of the Ossetians, even reached North Africa and Spain.

speech_bubbles.translation: Unite, brothers of the Ossetians! — the descendants of the Scythians, in the Caucasus.

name: Sogdian
region: Sogdia (modern Uzbekistan and Tajikistan, around Samarkand and Bukhara)
speakers_culture: Sogdian merchant communities along the Silk Road; the international traders of the ancient world
split_reason: Replaced by Arabic and then Turkic after the Islamic conquest and Turkic migrations. Its descendant Yaghnobi survives in Tajikistan.
interesting_fact: Sogdian was the international trade language of the Silk Road from ~300 CE to ~900 CE — the lingua franca of commerce from China to Iran. Sogdian merchants' letters from the 4th century CE have been found buried in towers along the Silk Road in China.

speech_bubbles.translation: I have sent many letters — Sogdian merchant letter from China, ~313 CE. The voice of the Silk Road.

name: Proto-Indo-Aryan
region: Central Asia / Bactria-Margiana region, moving into the Indian subcontinent
speakers_culture: Proto-Indo-Aryan peoples migrating through the Hindu Kush into the Indian subcontinent
split_reason: Migration into the Indian subcontinent produced Vedic Sanskrit and then the Prakrits, which evolved into the hundreds of modern Indo-Aryan languages.
interesting_fact: A striking anomaly: the Mitanni kingdom in Syria (~1500 BCE) had rulers with Sanskrit names and worshipped Vedic gods (Mitra, Varuna, Indra). This means Proto-Indo-Aryans passed through the Near East before some continued into India.

name: Vedic Sanskrit
region: Northern Indian subcontinent (Indus Valley, later Ganges plain)
speakers_culture: Vedic Aryan peoples; priestly (Brahmin) class who transmitted the Rigveda orally with extraordinary precision
split_reason: Evolved into Classical Sanskrit as codified by Pāṇini (~400 BCE). Vedic is older and less regular than Classical.
interesting_fact: The Rigveda (~1500–1200 BCE) was transmitted purely orally for 3,000 years with essentially no change — a feat of memorisation with no parallel in human history. The oral tradition used multiple cross-checking recitation methods to prevent any error.

sample_words.meaning: I praise Agni, the household priest — first line of the Rigveda

speech_bubbles.translation: I praise Agni, the household priest — first line of the Rigveda, ~1500 BCE. Memorised without writing for 3,000 years.

name: Classical Sanskrit

parent_influence.vedic-sanskrit: direct descent; codified and regularised by Pāṇini's grammar (~400 BCE), the most complete grammar of any ancient language

region: Indian subcontinent (used as a pan-Indian literary/religious language across all regions)
speakers_culture: Pan-Indian literary and religious tradition; Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism all use Sanskrit texts
split_reason: Sanskrit never died as a liturgical language but ceased to be a vernacular. The vernacular Prakrits evolved into all modern Indo-Aryan languages.
interesting_fact: Pāṇini's grammar of Sanskrit (~400 BCE), the Aṣṭādhyāyī, is considered the most complete and elegant grammar of any language ever written. Linguists in the 19th century used it as a model when trying to describe Indo-European linguistics scientifically.

sample_words.meaning: cosmic order, duty, righteousness (loaned into English)
action and its consequences (loaned into English)

speech_bubbles.translation: I am Brahman (the ultimate reality) — Upanishadic mahavakya (great saying), ~800 BCE.

name: Pali

parent_influence.vedic-sanskrit: direct descent via an early Prakrit; Pāli is a simplified Middle Indo-Aryan language based on a western Prakritic dialect

date_range: ~300 BCE–present (as canonical language of Theravada Buddhism)
region: Sri Lanka, Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, Laos (wherever Theravada Buddhism spread)
speakers_culture: Theravada Buddhist monastic communities across Southeast Asia and Sri Lanka
split_reason: Never a vernacular language; used solely as the canonical language of the Pali Canon (Tiṱpiṭaka), the oldest complete Buddhist scripture.
interesting_fact: The Pali Canon is the oldest complete collection of Buddhist scriptures and one of the longest texts in any ancient language — approximately 11 times longer than the Bible. It was transmitted orally for hundreds of years before being written down in Sri Lanka ~29 BCE.

sample_words.meaning: nirvana (liberation from suffering; loaned via Sanskrit into English)

speech_bubbles.translation: May all beings be happy — the Buddhist lovingkindness (metta) blessing.

name: Hindi / Urdu (Hindustani)

parent_influence.classical-sanskrit: grammatical structure and core vocabulary via Shauraseni Prakrit and Khari Boli
parent_influence.arabic: massive Arabic vocabulary (especially in Urdu via Persian)
parent_influence.modern-persian: Persian vocabulary layer, especially in Urdu
parent_influence.turkish: administrative vocabulary from the Mughal era

region: India (official in 9 states) and Pakistan (national language)
speakers_culture: ~600 million native speakers — the language with the most native speakers on Earth, or second (after Mandarin) depending on counting methodology
interesting_fact: Hindi and Urdu are the same spoken language — Hindustani — written in different scripts and with different vocabulary prestige layers. A Hindi speaker writing in Devanagari and an Urdu speaker writing in Nastaliq can have a conversation but cannot read each other's writing.

sample_words.meaning: hello (Hindi uses Sanskrit greeting; Urdu uses Arabic/Islamic)

speech_bubbles.original: सारे जहाँ से अच्छा हिन्دوस्तान हमारा
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Sāre jahāṃ se acchā Hindustān hamārā
speech_bubbles.translation: Better than all the world is our Hindustan — Iqbal, 1904. Written in Urdu, memorised in Hindi.

name: Bengali

parent_influence.classical-sanskrit: direct descent via Magadhi Prakrit and Apabhramsha

speakers_culture: ~230 million native speakers — the 6th most spoken language in the world
interesting_fact: Rabindranath Tagore — the first non-European to win the Nobel Prize in Literature (1913) — wrote in Bengali. His Bengali songs (Rabindra Sangeet) form such a vast cultural institution that the national anthems of both India and Bangladesh were written by him.

sample_words.meaning: My Golden Bengal — first words of the Bangladeshi national anthem (written by Tagore)

speech_bubbles.original: আমার সোনার বাংলা, আমি তোমায় ভালোবাসি
speech_bubbles.romanisation: Āmār soner Bānglā, āmi tomāy bhālobāsi
speech_bubbles.translation: My Golden Bengal, I love you — Tagore, 1905. The national anthem of Bangladesh.

name: Punjabi
region: Punjab (India and Pakistan); largest Punjabi-speaking population in Pakistan
speakers_culture: ~100–130 million speakers; also a large diaspora in UK, Canada, and Australia
interesting_fact: Punjabi is the liturgical language of Sikhism — the Guru Granth Sahib (the Sikh holy scripture) is written in Punjabi using the Gurmukhi script, specially developed for this purpose. Like Hindi/Urdu, Punjabi is written in two different scripts in India (Gurmukhi) and Pakistan (Shahmukhi).

sample_words.meaning: hello (Sikh greeting: 'The timeless Lord is Truth')

speech_bubbles.translation: There is one God — first line of the Guru Granth Sahib, Sikhism's scripture. Written in Punjabi.

name: Gujarati
region: Gujarat, India; large diaspora in UK, USA, East Africa
speakers_culture: ~60 million speakers; Jain religious texts and Gandhi's writings are in Gujarati
interesting_fact: Mahatma Gandhi's mother tongue was Gujarati. His vision of non-violent resistance (ahimsa) drew from Jainism, a religion whose canonical texts are partially written in Gujarati's ancestor languages.

speech_bubbles.translation: Gujarati and Gujarat are full of life.

name: Marathi
speakers_culture: ~83 million speakers
interesting_fact: Marathi has a rich literary tradition dating to the 13th century CE. The Dnyaneshwari (~1290 CE) — a commentary on the Bhagavad Gita — is one of the oldest major works in Marathi, written when the author was reportedly 16 years old.

speech_bubbles.translation: Victory to Maharashtra! — a language with 1,000 years of literature.

name: Nepali
speakers_culture: ~17 million native speakers; lingua franca of Nepal with 126 other languages
interesting_fact: Nepali is the native language of only about half the population of Nepal — the rest speak one of 126 other languages. It functions as Nepal's lingua franca, similar to how Swahili functions in East Africa.

speech_bubbles.translation: The motherland is greater than heaven — Sanskrit-origin saying, now Nepal's national motto.

name: Sinhala

parent_influence.classical-sanskrit: direct descent via Sinhala Prakrit; migrated to Sri Lanka from northern India ~500 BCE

speakers_culture: ~17 million native speakers; the Sinhalese Buddhist majority of Sri Lanka
interesting_fact: Sinhala is the most geographically isolated Indo-Aryan language — separated from all other Indo-Aryan languages by the Dravidian-speaking Tamil Nadu region and the Palk Strait. This isolation since ~500 BCE produced a completely distinctive branch.

speech_bubbles.translation: Long life to you, Sri Lanka — an Indo-Aryan language on a tropical island, isolated for 2,500 years.

name: Romani

parent_influence.classical-sanskrit: direct descent via Central Indo-Aryan (closest to Rajasthani/Hindi group); linguistic evidence proves origin in the Indian subcontinent
parent_influence.persian: Persian vocabulary layer from migration through Iran
parent_influence.greek: Greek vocabulary layer from centuries in the Byzantine world
parent_influence.modern-german: German and other European vocabulary from western migration

region: Dispersed across Europe (especially Eastern Europe), with communities worldwide
speakers_culture: ~3–5 million speakers; Roma people of Europe; historically persecuted; lost ~500,000 in the Holocaust (Porrajmos)
interesting_fact: Romani's Indian origin was only proven scientifically in the 18th century when a Hungarian student noticed similarities between Romani and Sanskrit. The Roma had lived in Europe for 500 years without Europeans knowing they came from India.

sample_words.meaning: day (from Sanskrit 'div', same root as Latin 'dies')

speech_bubbles.translation: Our days are blessed — Romani spoken across Europe, rooted in India.

name: Proto-Anatolian

parent_influence.proto-indo-european: direct descent; the first branch to split from PIE, around 3500 BCE

speakers_culture: Early Anatolian IE peoples; ancestors of the Hittites, Luwians, and Lydians
split_reason: Diverged into Hittite, Luwian, Lydian, Lycian, and other Anatolian languages as groups settled different parts of Anatolia.
interesting_fact: Anatolian languages are so old that they preserve features of PIE lost in all other branches — including a laryngeal consonant (/h/) that existed in PIE but disappeared elsewhere. Hittite proved the laryngeal theory that linguists had hypothesised from reconstructed evidence alone.

name: Hittite
region: Hittite Empire (central Anatolia, modern Turkey; also northern Syria and Levant at peak)
speakers_culture: Hittites — one of the great Bronze Age powers; rivals of Egypt and Babylon
split_reason: The Bronze Age Collapse (~1200 BCE) destroyed the Hittite Empire. The language died with it.
interesting_fact: The world's oldest surviving peace treaty was written in Hittite — the Treaty of Kadesh (1259 BCE) between the Hittite king Hattusili III and Ramesses II of Egypt. A copy hangs at the United Nations headquarters in New York.

sample_words.meaning: water (cf. English 'water', Greek 'hudor'; proving common PIE origin)

speech_bubbles.translation: Then the king put his hand... — Hittite royal chronicle, ~1400 BCE.

name: Luwian
region: Western and southern Anatolia, northern Syria
speakers_culture: Luwian peoples of the western Anatolian coast and post-Hittite Neo-Hittite states
split_reason: Survived the Bronze Age Collapse longer than Hittite; the Neo-Hittite city-states used Luwian hieroglyphs until absorbed by Assyria (~700 BCE).
interesting_fact: Luwian was written in two scripts: cuneiform (borrowed from Mesopotamia) and its own hieroglyphic script. Luwian hieroglyphs are completely unrelated to Egyptian hieroglyphics — an independent invention.

sample_words.meaning: the storm god — chief deity of the Luwians

speech_bubbles.translation: The Storm God commands. — Luwian hieroglyphic royal formula, ~1000 BCE.

name: Lydian
speakers_culture: The Lydians — famed as the inventors of coinage and ruled by Croesus (of 'rich as Croesus' fame)
split_reason: Conquered by Cyrus the Great of Persia in 547 BCE. Gradually replaced by Greek during the Hellenistic period.
interesting_fact: The Lydians invented coinage around 600 BCE — the first standardised metal coins in the world. Their king Croesus became the byword for wealth. The language died; the invention changed every economy on Earth.

speech_bubbles.translation: Croesus of Lydia — a name meaning incredible wealth, inscribed on coins.

name: Tocharian A

parent_influence.proto-indo-european: direct descent; Tocharian is a separate IE branch, probably branching early

speakers_culture: Tocharian-speaking Buddhist monks and merchants of the Tarim Basin oasis cities
split_reason: Replaced by Turkic languages as Uyghur and other Turkic peoples settled the Tarim Basin. The manuscripts were rediscovered in the early 20th century.
interesting_fact: Despite being spoken in China, Tocharian is more closely related to Celtic and Italic than to Indo-Iranian — a paradox. Tocharian speakers must have migrated through the steppe and east across Central Asia very early, before other branches had fully diverged.

speech_bubbles.translation: Many flowers are to be seen — a Tocharian A verse, ~700 CE. Found in a desert cave.

name: Tocharian B

parent_influence.proto-indo-european: direct descent; sibling of Tocharian A

region: Tarim Basin (modern Xinjiang, China) — western oasis cities (Kucha region)
speakers_culture: Kushean Buddhist kingdom merchants and clergy; western Tarim Basin
split_reason: Same fate as Tocharian A; replaced by Uyghur Turkic.
interesting_fact: Tocharian B had more surviving manuscripts than Tocharian A and was used as a trade language along the Silk Road. The two Tocharian languages were not mutually intelligible despite being close relatives.

sample_words.meaning: five (cf. Latin 'quinque' but showing different development)

speech_bubbles.translation: The city of Kucha — silk road merchant records, ~700 CE.

name: Thracian

parent_influence.proto-indo-european: direct descent; possibly related to Dacian and Phrygian but classification disputed

region: Thrace (modern Bulgaria, northern Greece, northwestern Turkey)
speakers_culture: The Thracians — warriors, horsemen, and neighbours of the Greeks. Spartacus was Thracian.
split_reason: Replaced by Greek and Latin following Roman conquest. Extinct by ~500 CE.
interesting_fact: Spartacus, who led the Third Servile War (73–71 BCE) against Rome, was a Thracian. We know almost nothing of his language — Thracian is one of the least-documented IE languages, known only from personal names and a handful of inscriptions.

sample_words.meaning: a major Thracian deity (name etymology disputed)

speech_bubbles.translation: ...the horseman... — Thracian votive inscription, ~300 BCE. One of few surviving phrases.

name: Phrygian

parent_influence.proto-indo-european: direct descent; possibly related to Greek and Armenian

speakers_culture: Phrygians — a major Bronze Age Anatolian people; their king Midas of the 'golden touch' legend was historical
split_reason: Replaced by Greek following Alexander's conquests. Extinct by ~500 CE.
interesting_fact: King Midas of the golden touch myth was a real Phrygian king. The Egyptians and Assyrians both recorded him. His tomb at Gordion may have been found — though archaeologists debate this. The Gordian Knot was also Phrygian.

sample_words.meaning: father (one of the universal 'babbling' words found across many IE languages)

speech_bubbles.translation: Whoever damages this tomb... — Phrygian tomb curse inscription, ~6th century BCE.
